title: Osteoarthritis
section: Disorders of the Joints and Adjacent Tissues

items.title: Prevalence and Risk Factors of Osteoarthritis
items.content: Osteoarthritis (OA) is the most common type of arthritis, especially prevalent in the elderly. Its occurrence is rising due to aging populations and increasing obesity rates.
keyPoints: OA is a leading cause of disability in the elderly.
Commonly affected joints include hip, knee, first MTP joint, cervical and lumbosacral spine, distal/proximal interphalangeal joints, and base of thumb.
Joints like wrist, elbow, and ankle are usually spared.
Prevalence of symptomatic OA in knees is ~12% for persons age ≥60 and 6% for adults age ≥30 in the U.S.
Symptomatic hip OA occurs at roughly one-third the rate of knee OA.
Painful hand OA affects ~10% of elderly individuals, despite high prevalence of radiographic changes.
OA is uncommon in adults aged <40 and highly prevalent in those >60.
OA is more common in women than men among middle-aged and elderly persons.

items.title: Diagnosis and Clinical Significance of Osteoarthritis
items.content: OA can be diagnosed based on structural abnormalities or symptoms. Structural changes are nearly universal in the elderly, but symptomatic OA is more clinically relevant.
keyPoints: Structural changes include cartilage loss (joint space loss) and osteophytes.
Many with x-ray evidence of OA have no joint symptoms; clinical focus is on symptomatic OA.
Symptomatic knee OA: pain on most days of a recent month plus x-ray evidence in that knee.
X-ray evidence of OA is common in lower back and neck, but not directly linked to back or neck pain.
Back pain and neck pain are treated separately from OA.

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DEFINITION | JOINT PROTECTIVE MECHANISMS AND THEIR FAILURE
items.title: Prevalence and Anatomic Predispositions in Osteoarthritis
items.content: Osteoarthritis (OA) prevalence increases with age, particularly in developed countries. Hip OA is notably rare among Chinese populations compared to whites due to anatomic differences that predispose white hips more to OA. However, knee OA is equally prevalent or more common in Chinese populations than in U.S. whites, contributing significantly to disability, especially in rural China.
keyPoints: OA prevalence increases with aging populations, particularly in developed countries.
Hip OA is rare in Chinese populations and immigrants from China to the United States due to anatomic differences.
Knee OA is at least as common in Chinese populations as in U.S. whites, representing a major cause of disability.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DEFINITION | JOINT PROTECTIVE MECHANISMS AND THEIR FAILURE

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DEFINITION | JOINT PROTECTIVE MECHANISMS AND THEIR FAILURE
items.title: Mechanisms and Failure of Joint Protection in Osteoarthritis
items.content: Joint protection mechanisms include the joint capsule, ligaments, muscles, sensory afferents, and synovial fluid. These protectors prevent excessive motion, reduce friction, provide feedback for muscle tension, and distribute stress across joints. Failures such as nerve sectioning or ligament rupture can lead to rapid OA development by increasing joint injury risk.
keyPoints: Joint protectors include capsule, ligaments, muscles, sensory afferents, and synovial fluid.
Synovial fluid's lubrication depends on hyaluronic acid and lubricin, which diminish after injury or inflammation.
Mechanoreceptors in ligaments provide feedback for muscle tension to protect joints.
Failure of joint protectors (e.g., nerve sectioning, ligament rupture) increases OA risk.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DEFINITION | JOINT PROTECTIVE MECHANISMS AND THEIR FAILURE

items.subtopic: CARTILAGE AND ITS ROLE IN JOINT FAILURE
items.title: Mechanisms of Cartilage Degradation in Osteoarthritis
items.content: Osteoarthritis (OA) involves the degradation of cartilage, primarily due to imbalances between matrix synthesis and catabolism. Chondrocytes play a central role by altering gene expression under mechanical and osmotic stress, leading to increased production of inflammatory cytokines and matrix-degrading enzymes such as MMPs. This results in the depletion of aggrecan and disorganization of type 2 collagen, reducing cartilage's compressive stiffness.
keyPoints: Type 2 collagen provides tensile strength; aggrecan contributes to compressive stiffness via electrostatic repulsion.
Chondrocytes synthesize matrix components and produce enzymes like MMPs that degrade the matrix.
Mechanical stress induces chondrocytes to increase inflammatory cytokines and matrix-degrading enzymes.
OA cartilage shows depletion of aggrecan, disorganization of collagen, and loss of compressive stiffness.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · CARTILAGE AND ITS ROLE IN JOINT FAILURE

items.subtopic: CARTILAGE AND ITS ROLE IN JOINT FAILURE
items.title: Role of Inflammation in Osteoarthritis Progression
items.content: Inflammation significantly accelerates OA development and pain. Mechanoinflammation, triggered by mechanical stimuli, involves the synovium, cartilage, and bone releasing cytokines and chemokines that promote inflammation. Matrix fragments from cartilage stimulate the synovium to release inflammatory cytokines, which further induce chondrocytes to produce proinflammatory molecules, leading to matrix degradation.
keyPoints: Local inflammation accelerates OA progression and joint pain.
Mechanoinflammation involves mechanical stimuli inducing an inflammatory response.
Matrix fragments from cartilage stimulate the synovium to release cytokines.
Inflammatory cytokines induce chondrocytes to produce proinflammatory molecules, leading to matrix degradation.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · CARTILAGE AND ITS ROLE IN JOINT FAILURE

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS | SYSTEMIC RISK FACTORS THAT AFFECT JOINT VULNERABILITY
items.title: Age as a Potent Risk Factor for Osteoarthritis (OA)
items.content: Aging significantly increases joint vulnerability to OA due to decreased cartilage matrix synthesis, muscle weakening, slower sensory nerve input, and ligament stretching.
keyPoints: Radiographic evidence of OA is rare in individuals aged <40; however, >50% of persons aged >70 have OA in some joints like the hands.
Aging leads to reduced cartilage matrix synthesis with dynamic loading, resulting in thinner cartilage and higher shear stress.
Muscles bridging joints become weaker and slower to respond with age.
Sensory nerve input slows, affecting feedback loops of mechanoreceptors related to muscle and tendon tension.
Ligaments stretch with age, reducing their ability to absorb impulses.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · RISK FACTORS | SYSTEMIC RISK FACTORS THAT AFFECT JOINT VULNERABILITY | HERITABILITY AND GENETICS AND THEIR RELATION TO JOINT VULNERABILITY

items.subtopic: HERITABILITY AND GENETICS AND THEIR RELATION TO JOINT VULNERABILITY
items.title: Heritability of Osteoarthritis and the Role of GDF5
items.content: OA heritability is joint-specific, with high genetic influence in hips or hands (60-65%) but lower in knees (up to 30%). The genetic variant at the GDF5 locus affects OA risk by influencing joint shape.
keyPoints: Nearly 60–65% of hip or hand OA may be attributed to shared genetics within a family.
Heritability of knee OA is, at most, 30%, with some studies suggesting no heritability.
GDF5 genetic variant affects epigenetic regulation and reduces GDF5 expression, influencing joint shape and vulnerability.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · RISK FACTORS | SYSTEMIC RISK FACTORS THAT AFFECT JOINT VULNERABILITY | HERITABILITY AND GENETICS AND THEIR RELATION TO JOINT VULNERABILITY

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS IN THE JOINT ENVIRONMENT
items.title: Anatomic Abnormalities and Their Role in Osteoarthritis Development
items.content: Certain developmental abnormalities and injuries can lead to anatomic distortions that predispose joints to osteoarthritis (OA). Conditions like congenital dysplasia, Legg-Perthes disease, and slipped capital femoral epiphysis alter joint anatomy, leading to uneven load distribution and increased focal stress. These changes often result in OA later in life, with the timing of onset depending on the severity of the abnormalities.
keyPoints: Congenital dysplasia predominantly affects girls; other hip abnormalities more common in boys.
Severe anatomic abnormalities can lead to hip OA in young adulthood; mild abnormalities may result in middle-aged onset.
Femoroacetabular impingement during adolescence can cause cartilage and labral damage, increasing later-life risk of hip OA.
Major injuries like fractures or avascular necrosis can create anatomic irregularities leading to OA.
Meniscal tears increase with age and contribute to adjacent cartilage damage and accelerated OA.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · RISK FACTORS IN THE JOINT ENVIRONMENT

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS IN THE JOINT ENVIRONMENT
items.title: Impact of Malalignment and Muscle Weakness on Osteoarthritis Progression
items.content: Malalignment across joints, particularly in the knee, significantly increases the risk of osteoarthritis by concentrating stress on specific areas of cartilage. Varus or valgus malalignments predispose to rapid cartilage loss in respective compartments. Additionally, weakness in quadriceps muscles is associated with an increased risk of painful OA, especially in women.
keyPoints: Varus (bow-legged) alignment increases medial knee compartment stress; valgus (knock-kneed) affects the lateral compartment.
Malalignment leads to focal cartilage breakdown and bone marrow lesions visible on MRI.
Severely malaligned knees may progress to OA regardless of other risk factors.
Quadriceps muscle weakness, particularly in women, heightens the risk of painful knee OA.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · RISK FACTORS IN THE JOINT ENVIRONMENT

items.title: Impact of Obesity on Knee Osteoarthritis (OA)
items.content: Obesity significantly increases the risk and severity of knee OA due to the mechanical loading it imposes on weight-bearing joints. The force transmitted across the knee during single-leg stance is three to six times body weight, which exacerbates joint stress in overweight individuals.
keyPoints: Obesity is a potent risk factor for knee OA and less so for hip OA.
The relationship between weight and disease risk is linear; each additional pound increases risk.
Weight loss greater than 10% reduces cartilage loss and alleviates pain.
High levels of leptin are associated with increased pain severity in OA, regardless of the joint.

items.title: Repetitive Joint Use and Exercise Impact on Osteoarthritis Risk
items.content: Occupational repetitive use of joints can lead to OA due to prolonged stress without adequate muscle protection. Conversely, regular exercise does not consistently increase OA risk but may exacerbate it in individuals with pre-existing joint injuries.
keyPoints: Occupational repetitive tasks heighten the risk of developing OA in frequently used joints.
Muscle exhaustion from long workdays reduces their protective effect on joints.
Long-term studies show no consistent association between exercise and increased OA risk for most people.
Individuals with prior joint injuries, especially major knee injuries, are at higher risk of progressive OA with certain exercises like running.
Elite runners have high risks of both knee and hip OA; recreational runners have a modest increased risk of hip OA.

items.title: Mechanism of Cartilage Damage in Osteoarthritis
items.content: In osteoarthritis, chondrocytes undergo mitosis and clustering after cartilage injury. This results in high metabolic activity but leads to proteoglycan depletion due to greater catabolic than synthetic activity. Damaged collagen matrix exposes negative charges of proteoglycans, causing cartilage swelling from ionic attraction to water molecules. Consequently, the cartilage loses its ability to rebound after loading and becomes more susceptible to further injury.
keyPoints: Chondrocytes undergo mitosis and clustering post-injury.
Catabolic activity exceeds synthetic activity leading to proteoglycan depletion.
Damaged collagen matrix exposes negative charges, causing cartilage swelling.
Cartilage loses its ability to rebound after loading.

items.title: Subchondral Bone and Synovial Changes in Osteoarthritis
items.content: In osteoarthritis, subchondral bone undergoes changes due to growth factors and cytokines activating osteoclasts and osteoblasts. This results in thickening of the subchondral plate before cartilage ulceration. Bone trauma during joint loading can lead to microcracks and osteonecrosis. At the joint margin, osteophytes form from ossified new cartilage outgrowths due to neurovascular invasion. The synovium becomes edematous and inflamed in OA, with macrophages migrating into the tissue and proliferating cells secreting inflammatory cytokines that activate chondrocytes to produce matrix-degrading enzymes.
keyPoints: Subchondral bone thickens due to osteoclasts and osteoblast activation before cartilage ulceration.
Bone trauma can lead to microcracks and osteonecrosis.
Osteophytes form at joint margins from ossified new cartilage outgrowths.
Synovium becomes edematous and inflamed, secreting inflammatory cytokines.

items.title: Pain in Osteoarthritis: Beyond Cartilage Loss
items.content: In osteoarthritis (OA), pain is not primarily due to cartilage loss as healthy cartilage lacks nerve supply. Instead, pain arises from innervated structures such as the synovium, ligaments, joint capsule, muscles, and subchondral bone. In later stages of OA, neurovascular invasion into compromised cartilage may exacerbate pain.
keyPoints: Healthy cartilage is anerural; thus, its loss alone does not cause much pain.
Pain in OA likely arises from structures outside the cartilage like synovium and subchondral bone.
In later stages of OA, neurovascular invasion into compromised cartilage contributes to pain.

items.title: Mechanisms of Pain Sensitization in Osteoarthritis
items.content: Pain in osteoarthritis can become constant and present at rest due to central and peripheral sensitization. Peripheral nociceptors may become more responsive (peripheral sensitization), while there is increased facilitation of central ascending nociceptive signaling (central sensitization). Insufficient descending inhibitory modulation also contributes, potentially leading to chronic pain.
keyPoints: Pain in OA can transition from activity-induced to constant due to nervous system changes.
Peripheral sensitization involves heightened responsiveness of peripheral nociceptors.
Central sensitization refers to increased facilitation of central ascending nociceptive signaling.
Insufficient descending inhibitory modulation contributes to chronic pain development.

items.title: Activity-Related Joint Pain in Osteoarthritis (OA)
items.content: In the early stages of OA, joint pain is primarily activity-related and occurs during or just after joint use. It gradually resolves but can become continuous as the disease progresses. Morning stiffness is usually brief (<30 min).
keyPoints: Pain comes on with joint use and resolves gradually.
Early pain is episodic and triggered by overuse, e.g., knee OA from long runs.
As OA progresses, pain becomes continuous and may be bothersome at night.
Morning stiffness in affected joints is usually brief (<30 min).

items.title: Differential Diagnosis and Evaluation of Chronic Knee Pain in OA
items.content: OA is the most common cause of chronic knee pain in persons aged >45. Differential diagnosis includes inflammatory arthritis, bursitis, and other joint pathologies. Physical examination should focus on tenderness over or outside the joint line. Prominent nocturnal pain without end-stage OA requires distinct workup.
keyPoints: OA is the most common cause of chronic knee pain in those aged >45.
Inflammatory arthritis likely if prolonged morning stiffness and multiple joints are affected.
Bursitis, especially anserine bursitis, can be a common cause of knee pain.
Physical exam: assess tenderness over or outside joint line; nocturnal pain without end-stage OA needs further evaluation.

items.title: Multimodality Approach to Osteoarthritis Management
items.content: The management of osteoarthritis (OA) involves a comprehensive, multimodal approach that addresses pain and functional impairment through both physical modalities and pharmacologic treatments. Foundational elements include patient education and self-management strategies.
keyPoints: Goals: Alleviate pain and minimize loss of physical function.
Approach: Multimodality involving physical modalities and pharmacologic elements.
Foundational Elements: Patient education, weight management, physical activity, exercise, and self-management strategies.

items.title: Treatment Strategies for Knee Osteoarthritis
items.content: Knee osteoarthritis treatment has been more extensively evaluated compared to other joints. Management involves both physical modalities and pharmacotherapy, especially for patients with ongoing, disabling pain.
keyPoints: Focus: Treatment strategies are primarily based on knee OA due to extensive evaluation.
Management: Involves physical modalities and pharmacotherapy.
Specific Recommendations: Tailored for knee OA but principles apply to other joints.

items.title: Avoiding Painful Activities Alleviates Symptoms
items.content: For osteoarthritis (OA), avoiding activities that precipitate pain can significantly alleviate symptoms. For instance, a middle-aged patient with knee OA may find relief by switching from long-distance running to less demanding weight-bearing activities.
keyPoints: Avoiding painful activities reduces joint overload and alleviates symptoms.
Example: Middle-aged patients with knee OA benefit from less demanding weight-bearing activities.

items.title: Weight Loss as a Central Strategy for Knee OA
items.content: Weight loss is crucial for overweight or obese individuals with knee OA, as each pound lost reduces load on the knees and hips, potentially relieving pain and slowing disease progression.
keyPoints: Each pound of weight loss unloads both knees and hips.
Bariatric surgery and GLP1R agonists can reduce pain and may slow structural disease progression.

items.title: Exercise Improves Pain and Physical Function in OA
items.content: Exercise, including aerobic and resistance training, is recommended for knee and hip OA to lessen pain and improve physical function. Low-impact exercises are often better tolerated.
keyPoints: Exercise lessens pain and improves physical function in knee and hip OA.
Low-impact exercises like water aerobics are well-tolerated.
High-intensity strengthening is not more effective than less aggressive regimens.

items.title: Enhancing Adherence in Exercise Prescriptions for Knee OA
items.content: Adherence to exercise regimens over the long term is a significant challenge, with less than half of patients continuing regular exercise at one year. A previous history of successful exercise is a strong predictor of continued adherence.
keyPoints: From a third to over half of knee OA patients stop exercising by 6 months.
Less than 50% continue regular exercise at 1 year.
Previous personal history of successful exercise predicts continued adherence.
Reinforce exercise prescriptions at clinic visits and use mobile health technologies for encouragement.

items.title: Correcting Malalignment in Knee OA
items.content: Malalignment, particularly varus-valgus malalignment, increases joint stress and can lead to disease progression. Corrective measures like bracing or surgery may relieve pain if the patient is willing and able to use them properly.
keyPoints: Varus-valgus malalignment increases stress across the knee joint.
Correcting malalignment with braces or surgery can relieve pain.
Braces are effective but often not worn due to discomfort or slippage, especially in obese patients.
Shoes modified with rubber hemispheres can alter alignment and reduce pain if used daily over several months.

items.title: Acetaminophen vs. NSAIDs for OA Pain Management
items.content: Acetaminophen is often used for mild osteoarthritis (OA) pain, offering a safer alternative to NSAIDs for some patients due to its lower risk of gastrointestinal side effects.
keyPoints: Acetaminophen provides small and not clinically meaningful improvement in OA symptoms.
Oral NSAIDs produce ~30% greater improvement in pain than high-dose acetaminophen.
Topical NSAIDs have fewer GI side effects compared to oral NSAIDs.

items.title: NSAID Administration and Side Effects
items.content: Oral NSAIDs are effective for moderate to severe OA pain but carry significant risks, including gastrointestinal toxicity. Topical NSAIDs offer a safer profile with fewer systemic effects.
keyPoints: Topical NSAIDs cause local skin irritation but have far fewer GI side effects than oral NSAIDs.
Common oral NSAID side effects include upper GI toxicity and cardiovascular events.
Celecoxib (≤200 mg/d) does not elevate cardiovascular risk, unlike other conventional NSAIDs.

items.title: Intraarticular Injections for OA
items.content: Glucocorticoid injections can provide pain relief in OA by reducing synovitis, while hyaluronic acid injections show limited efficacy. Repeated glucocorticoid injections may cause cartilage loss but do not significantly impact disease progression.
keyPoints: Glucocorticoid injections offer variable pain relief lasting up to several months.
Hyaluronic acid injections have little efficacy versus placebo in knee and hip OA.
Repeated glucocorticoid injections can lead to minor cartilage loss without increasing risk of disease progression.

items.title: Opioids: Limited Role and Risks in OA Management
items.content: Opioids provide only modest short-term pain relief for hip or knee osteoarthritis (OA) without evidence of long-term benefit. Due to the risk of opioid dependency, they should be avoided.
keyPoints: Opioids have modest short-term efficacy in treating OA pain
No evidence of long-term benefit from opioids in OA
Concerns about opioid dependency necessitate avoidance

items.title: Duloxetine and Biologics: Specific Uses in OA Treatment
items.content: Duloxetine is FDA-approved for OA, particularly effective when knee pain is part of widespread pain. Biologic agents generally show no benefit for OA except in specific cases like hand OA treated with prednisolone or methotrexate.
keyPoints: Duloxetine is FDA approved for OA
Effective for knee pain within a syndrome of widespread pain
Biologics mostly ineffective for OA, exceptions include: 6 weeks of prednisolone and 6 months of methotrexate for hand OA
GLP1R agonists reduced knee pain in obese patients

items.question: Which joints are commonly affected by osteoarthritis?
options: Hip, knee, first metatarsal phalangeal joint, cervical and lumbosacral spine
items.explanation: OA commonly affects the hip, knee, first MTP joint, and cervical and lumbosacral spine.

items.question: Which of the following is a major risk factor contributing to the increased prevalence of osteoarthritis?
items.explanation: Obesity is a major risk factor for OA, along with the aging of Western populations.

items.question: Which joints are usually spared from osteoarthritis?
items.explanation: The wrist, elbow, and ankle are usually spared from OA.

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DEFINITION | JOINT PROTECTIVE MECHANISMS AND THEIR FAILURE
items.question: Which factor is NOT mentioned as contributing to the higher prevalence of hip osteoarthritis (OA) in white populations compared to Chinese populations?
options: Anatomic differences between Chinese and white hips.
Higher rates of joint injury in white populations.
items.explanation: The passage mentions anatomic differences as a factor, but does not mention higher rates of joint injury or lifestyle/genetic factors specifically for hip OA prevalence in whites versus Chinese.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DEFINITION | JOINT PROTECTIVE MECHANISMS AND THEIR FAILURE

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DEFINITION | JOINT PROTECTIVE MECHANISMS AND THEIR FAILURE
items.question: What is the primary pathological change that defines osteoarthritis (OA)?
items.explanation: The passage states that the pathologic sine qua non of OA is hyaline articular cartilage loss.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DEFINITION | JOINT PROTECTIVE MECHANISMS AND THEIR FAILURE

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DEFINITION | JOINT PROTECTIVE MECHANISMS AND THEIR FAILURE
items.question: Which joint protector's failure can lead to rapid development of osteoarthritis (OA) in humans?
items.explanation: The passage explains that OA develops rapidly when a sensory nerve to the joint is sectioned, indicating the importance of mechanoreceptor sensory nerves as protectors.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DEFINITION | JOINT PROTECTIVE MECHANISMS AND THEIR FAILURE

items.subtopic: CARTILAGE AND ITS ROLE IN JOINT FAILURE
items.question: Which macromolecule provides tensile strength to cartilage?
items.explanation: Type 2 collagen is responsible for providing tensile strength to cartilage.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · CARTILAGE AND ITS ROLE IN JOINT FAILURE

items.subtopic: CARTILAGE AND ITS ROLE IN JOINT FAILURE
items.question: What contributes to the compressive stiffness of normal cartilage?
items.explanation: The electrostatic repulsion of the negatively charged aggrecan molecules contributes to cartilage's compressive stiffness.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · CARTILAGE AND ITS ROLE IN JOINT FAILURE

items.subtopic: CARTILAGE AND ITS ROLE IN JOINT FAILURE
items.question: What is a critical enzyme involved in the breakdown of the cartilage matrix?
items.explanation: Matrix metalloproteinases, especially collagenases, are critical enzymes in the breakdown of cartilage matrix.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · CARTILAGE AND ITS ROLE IN JOINT FAILURE

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS | SYSTEMIC RISK FACTORS THAT AFFECT JOINT VULNERABILITY | HERITABILITY AND GENETICS AND THEIR RELATION TO JOINT VULNERABILITY
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a mechanism by which aging increases joint vulnerability to osteoarthritis (OA)?
options: Aging cartilage becomes more responsive to dynamic loading.
Muscles that bridge joints become weaker with age.
Sensory nerve input slows, affecting feedback loops.
Ligaments stretch and become less able to absorb impulses.
items.explanation: Aging cartilage becomes less responsive to dynamic loading, not more.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · RISK FACTORS | SYSTEMIC RISK FACTORS THAT AFFECT JOINT VULNERABILITY | HERITABILITY AND GENETICS AND THEIR RELATION TO JOINT VULNERABILITY

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS | SYSTEMIC RISK FACTORS THAT AFFECT JOINT VULNERABILITY | HERITABILITY AND GENETICS AND THEIR RELATION TO JOINT VULNERABILITY
items.question: What is the heritability percentage of osteoarthritis in hips or hands attributed to shared genetics within a family?
items.explanation: Nearly 60–65% of OA in hips or hands may be attributed to shared genetics.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · RISK FACTORS | SYSTEMIC RISK FACTORS THAT AFFECT JOINT VULNERABILITY | HERITABILITY AND GENETICS AND THEIR RELATION TO JOINT VULNERABILITY

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS | SYSTEMIC RISK FACTORS THAT AFFECT JOINT VULNERABILITY | HERITABILITY AND GENETICS AND THEIR RELATION TO JOINT VULNERABILITY
items.question: Which genetic variant is known to increase the risk of osteoarthritis by affecting joint shape?
items.explanation: The genetic variant in GDF5 affects epigenetic regulation, influencing joint shape and OA risk.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · RISK FACTORS | SYSTEMIC RISK FACTORS THAT AFFECT JOINT VULNERABILITY | HERITABILITY AND GENETICS AND THEIR RELATION TO JOINT VULNERABILITY

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS IN THE JOINT ENVIRONMENT
items.question: Which developmental abnormality predominantly affects girls and can lead to osteoarthritis (OA) later in life?
items.explanation: Acetabular dysplasia, a mild form of congenital dysplasia, predominantly affects girls.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · RISK FACTORS IN THE JOINT ENVIRONMENT

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS IN THE JOINT ENVIRONMENT
items.question: What is the primary effect of malalignment across the knee joint?
options: Causes increased focal stress leading to cartilage breakdown
Improves load distribution across the joint
items.explanation: Malalignment increases stress on a focal area of cartilage, causing it to break down.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · RISK FACTORS IN THE JOINT ENVIRONMENT

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS IN THE JOINT ENVIRONMENT
items.question: Which condition is associated with an increased risk of osteoarthritis due to chronic meniscal tears?
options: Meniscal tears leading to adjacent cartilage damage
items.explanation: Chronic meniscal tears lead to adjacent cartilage damage and accelerated OA.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · RISK FACTORS IN THE JOINT ENVIRONMENT

items.question: Which factor is identified as a stronger risk for knee osteoarthritis (OA) in women compared to men?
items.explanation: Obesity is a stronger risk factor for knee OA in women than in men.

items.question: What effect does weight loss greater than 10% of body weight have on individuals with osteoarthritis?
options: Alleviates pain and reduces cartilage loss
Has no effect on OA symptoms
items.explanation: Weight loss greater than 10% of body weight reduces cartilage loss and alleviates pain.

items.question: Which type of exercise is associated with an increased risk of progressive knee OA in individuals who have sustained major knee injuries?
items.explanation: Running increases the risk of progressive knee OA in those with prior major knee injuries.

items.question: What is the primary reason for proteoglycan depletion around chondrocytes after cartilage injury?
options: Greater catabolic activity than synthetic activity
Decreased metabolic activity of chondrocyte clusters
Enhanced ionic attraction to water molecules
items.explanation: The net effect of high metabolic activity in chondrocyte clusters is proteoglycan depletion because catabolic activity exceeds synthetic activity.

items.question: What causes the swelling of cartilage after damage?
options: Exposure of negative charges on proteoglycans
items.explanation: The exposure of the negative charges of proteoglycans leads to swelling due to ionic attraction to water molecules.

items.question: What is a radiographic hallmark of osteoarthritis (OA)?
items.explanation: Osteophytes are an important radiographic hallmark of OA.

items.question: Which structure is NOT typically visualized by x-ray but can be a source of pain in osteoarthritis?
items.explanation: The synovium, along with ligaments and joint capsules, are innervated structures that are not visualized by x-ray but can be sources of pain in OA.

items.question: What is correlated with the presence and severity of knee pain according to MRI studies?
items.explanation: The presence of synovitis on MRI is correlated with the presence and severity of knee pain.

items.question: Which condition may lead to central sensitization in osteoarthritis?
items.explanation: Alterations in nervous system signaling can lead to central sensitization, contributing to more severe pain.

items.question: Which symptom is typically brief in the early stages of osteoarthritis?
options: Pain that occurs during joint use
Morning stiffness lasting less than 30 minutes
Mechanical symptoms like catching or locking
items.explanation: Morning stiffness in OA is usually brief, typically lasting less than 30 minutes.

items.question: What type of pain is common in the patellofemoral compartment of the knee due to osteoarthritis?
options: Pain with activities requiring knee extension
Pain when the knee is not bent at all
Pain with activities requiring knee flexion
items.explanation: Pain in the patellofemoral compartment often occurs with knee flexion, such as stair climbing.

items.question: What is a common cause of chronic knee pain that may respond to glucocorticoid injection?
items.explanation: Anserine bursitis is a common cause of chronic knee pain that may respond to glucocorticoid injection.

items.question: Which of the following is a primary goal in the treatment of osteoarthritis (OA)?
options: Alleviate pain and minimize loss of physical function
Increase joint inflammation to strengthen surrounding muscles
Promote joint laxity for better mobility
Prevent any form of physical activity
items.explanation: The primary goals are to alleviate pain and minimize loss of physical function.

items.question: What is foundational to the management of osteoarthritis?
options: Immediate surgical intervention for all patients
items.explanation: Foundational to OA management is patient education and self-management strategies.

items.question: Which joint's osteoarthritis treatments have been more completely evaluated?
items.explanation: Treatments for knee OA have been more completely evaluated than those for hip and hand OA.

items.question: Which of the following strategies is primarily recommended for unloading weight-bearing joints such as knees and hips in patients with osteoarthritis?
options: Using a brace or splint to redistribute load within the joint
Avoiding all forms of physical activity
Using a cane or crutch for partial weight bearing
items.explanation: Using a cane or crutch helps unload the affected joint during weight-bearing activities.

items.question: What is one of the main reasons for recommending exercise to patients with knee or hip osteoarthritis?
options: To increase high-impact loading on joints
To improve muscle strength and physical function
To avoid any form of weight-bearing activities
items.explanation: Exercise improves muscle strength, lessens pain, and enhances physical function.

items.question: What is a recommended strategy for managing hand osteoarthritis in the base of the thumb?
items.explanation: Splinting helps minimize pain by limiting motion in the affected joint.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT an effective exercise type for knee and hip osteoarthritis?
options: Resistance training focused on strengthening muscles across the joint
items.explanation: Range-of-motion exercises by themselves are unlikely to be effective.

items.question: Which factor is the strongest predictor of a patient's continued exercise in knee OA?
options: Previous personal history of successful exercise
Regular reinforcement by physicians at each clinic visit
Use of mobile health and wearable technologies
Combination of exercise with calorie restriction
items.explanation: A previous personal history of successful exercise is the strongest predictor of continued exercise.

items.question: What is a challenge in correcting varus-valgus malalignment with bracing?
options: Braces are universally effective for all patients
Patients often find it difficult to apply tape without skin irritation
Many patients are unwilling to wear realigning knee braces
Shoes modified with rubber hemispheres can correct alignment
items.explanation: Many patients are unwilling to wear a realigning knee brace.

items.question: What is the therapeutic effect of neoprene sleeves on knee OA pain?
options: Their effect on malalignment is questionable but they lessen pain
They provide prolonged pain relief similar to radiofrequency ablation
They are as effective as acupuncture in providing pain relief
items.explanation: Neoprene sleeves' effect on malalignment is questionable, but they do lessen pain.

items.question: Which of the following statements about acetaminophen's role in osteoarthritis treatment is correct?
options: Acetaminophen significantly alters the disease process.
Acetaminophen provides clinically meaningful pain relief for most patients with OA.
For a minority of patients, acetaminophen can adequately control symptoms without using more toxic drugs like oral NSAIDs.
Acetaminophen is the first-line treatment recommended for severe osteoarthritic pain.
items.explanation: Acetaminophen's effect in OA is small and not considered clinically meaningful, but it may control symptoms for a minority of patients without needing more toxic drugs like oral NSAIDs.

items.question: Which route of administration results in lower plasma concentrations when using topical NSAIDs compared to oral NSAIDs?
items.explanation: Topical NSAIDs absorbed through the skin have plasma concentrations an order of magnitude lower than those administered orally.

items.question: Which oral NSAID is considered safer for the stomach compared to others?
items.explanation: Certain oral agents like celecoxib and nabumetone are safer for the stomach than others.

items.question: What is a common side effect of NSAIDs related to kidney function?
items.explanation: NSAIDs have a predilection toward reversible renal insufficiency due to prostaglandin inhibition affecting kidney function.

items.question: Which medication is FDA-approved for osteoarthritis and may be particularly effective when knee pain is part of a syndrome of widespread pain?
items.explanation: Duloxetine is FDA-approved for OA and may be particularly efficacious when knee pain is part of a syndrome of widespread pain.

items.question: Which class of drugs should generally be avoided in the treatment of hip or knee osteoarthritis due to concerns about dependency and lack of long-term benefit?
items.explanation: Opioids have only modest short-term efficacy in treating pain in hip or knee OA without evidence of long-term benefit and should be avoided due to concerns about opioid dependency.

items.question: Which biologic agent showed a reduction in hand pain compared with placebo in trials for osteoarthritis?
items.explanation: Two randomized trials for hand OA, one of 6 weeks of prednisolone and the other of 6 months of methotrexate, demonstrated reductions in hand pain compared with placebo.

items.statement: Symptomatic osteoarthritis of the knee occurs in ~12% of persons aged ≥60 in the United States.
items.explanation: The passage states that symptomatic OA of the knee occurs in ~12% of persons age ≥60.

items.statement: Back pain and neck pain are directly tied to findings of osteoarthritis on x-ray.
items.explanation: The passage states that back pain and neck pain have not been tied to OA findings on x-ray.

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DEFINITION | JOINT PROTECTIVE MECHANISMS AND THEIR FAILURE
items.statement: Knee osteoarthritis is less common in Chinese populations compared to whites from the United States.
items.explanation: The passage states that knee OA is at least as common, if not more so, in Chinese as in whites.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DEFINITION | JOINT PROTECTIVE MECHANISMS AND THEIR FAILURE

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DEFINITION | JOINT PROTECTIVE MECHANISMS AND THEIR FAILURE
items.statement: Muscle contraction helps minimize focal stress across the joint by distributing impact broadly.
items.explanation: The passage describes how muscle contraction decelerates the joint and distributes impact, acting as a protector.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | DEFINITION | JOINT PROTECTIVE MECHANISMS AND THEIR FAILURE

items.subtopic: CARTILAGE AND ITS ROLE IN JOINT FAILURE
items.statement: Mechanical and osmotic stress on chondrocytes leads to decreased production of inflammatory cytokines.
items.explanation: Mechanical and osmotic stress induces chondrocytes to increase the production of inflammatory cytokines.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · CARTILAGE AND ITS ROLE IN JOINT FAILURE

items.subtopic: CARTILAGE AND ITS ROLE IN JOINT FAILURE
items.statement: Aging leads to a decline in synthetic capacity and an increase in proinflammatory mediators in articular chondrocytes.
items.explanation: With aging, articular chondrocytes develop a senescence-associated secretory phenotype with these characteristics.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · CARTILAGE AND ITS ROLE IN JOINT FAILURE

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS | SYSTEMIC RISK FACTORS THAT AFFECT JOINT VULNERABILITY | HERITABILITY AND GENETICS AND THEIR RELATION TO JOINT VULNERABILITY
items.statement: The heritability of knee osteoarthritis is higher than that of hip or hand osteoarthritis.
items.explanation: Heritability of knee OA is at most 30%, whereas for hips or hands it's 60-65%.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · RISK FACTORS | SYSTEMIC RISK FACTORS THAT AFFECT JOINT VULNERABILITY | HERITABILITY AND GENETICS AND THEIR RELATION TO JOINT VULNERABILITY

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS | SYSTEMIC RISK FACTORS THAT AFFECT JOINT VULNERABILITY | HERITABILITY AND GENETICS AND THEIR RELATION TO JOINT VULNERABILITY
items.statement: Older women have a higher risk of osteoarthritis in all joints compared to men.
items.explanation: Older women are at high risk for OA, particularly as they reach their sixth decade.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · RISK FACTORS | SYSTEMIC RISK FACTORS THAT AFFECT JOINT VULNERABILITY | HERITABILITY AND GENETICS AND THEIR RELATION TO JOINT VULNERABILITY

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS IN THE JOINT ENVIRONMENT
items.statement: Avascular necrosis can result in anatomic irregularities that contribute to osteoarthritis.
items.explanation: Avascular necrosis leads to collapse of dead bone at the articular surface, causing anatomic irregularities and subsequent OA.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · RISK FACTORS IN THE JOINT ENVIRONMENT

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS IN THE JOINT ENVIRONMENT
items.statement: High bone density decreases the risk of osteoarthritis.
items.explanation: High bone density increases the risk of a subtype of OA characterized by large osteophytes.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · RISK FACTORS IN THE JOINT ENVIRONMENT

items.statement: High levels of leptin are associated with increased pain severity in osteoarthritis regardless of the joint affected.
items.explanation: Leptin, an adipokine, is linked to increasing pain severity in OA irrespective of the joint.

items.statement: Long-term studies suggest a consistent association between exercise and increased risk of osteoarthritis.
items.explanation: There is no consistent association of exercise with OA risk in most persons.

items.statement: In osteoarthritis, the synovium always becomes inflamed and edematous.
items.explanation: The synovium can sometimes become edematous and inflamed in OA, but it is not always the case.

items.statement: Basic calcium phosphate crystals are present microscopically in most joints with end-stage osteoarthritis.
items.explanation: These crystals are indeed present microscopically in most joints with end-stage OA.

items.statement: Cartilage loss alone is a major source of pain in osteoarthritis.
items.explanation: Healthy cartilage is anerural, so cartilage loss alone does not cause much pain; pain arises from other structures.

items.statement: Bone marrow edema in osteoarthritis indicates the presence of microcracks and scar tissue.
items.explanation: Bone marrow edema signals microcracks and scar, which are consequences of trauma.

items.statement: Radiographs are routinely indicated in the initial workup for osteoarthritis.
items.explanation: Radiographs should only be ordered if joint pain and physical findings are atypical of OA or if pain persists after treatment.

items.statement: MRI is part of the routine diagnostic workup for osteoarthritis.
items.explanation: MRI is not indicative as part of the diagnostic workup for OA, despite revealing the extent of pathology.

items.statement: The principles of treatment are identical for osteoarthritis in all joints.
items.explanation: Although specific recommendations may differ, the principles of treatment are identical for OA in all joints.

items.statement: Patients with mild and intermittent symptoms always require pharmacotherapy.
items.explanation: Patients with mild and intermittent symptoms may need only symptomatic management without pharmacotherapy.

items.statement: Weight loss is a central strategy for managing knee osteoarthritis, particularly in overweight or obese patients.
items.explanation: Each pound of weight loss reduces load on knees and hips, alleviating pain.

items.statement: High-intensity strengthening exercises are more effective than less aggressive regimens for reducing knee pain in osteoarthritis.
items.explanation: Evidence suggests no additional benefit of high-intensity over less aggressive strengthening.

items.statement: Radiofrequency ablation of the nerves innervating the joint provides prolonged pain relief in knee OA.
items.explanation: Radiofrequency ablation has been shown to provide prolonged pain relief.

items.statement: Acupuncture produces significant pain relief compared to placebo needles in knee OA.
items.explanation: Acupuncture provides only modest pain relief compared to placebo.

items.statement: Topical NSAIDs are more efficacious than oral agents in treating osteoarthritic pain.
items.explanation: Generally, topical NSAIDs are slightly less efficacious than oral agents.

items.statement: Glucocorticoid injections can provide pain relief in osteoarthritis for up to several months.
items.explanation: Most patients experience pain relief lasting up to several months after glucocorticoid injections.

items.statement: GLP1R agonists have been shown to reduce knee pain in obese patients with osteoarthritis.
items.explanation: A large randomized trial demonstrated that GLP1R agonists reduced knee pain in obese patients.

items.statement: Biologic agents used for rheumatoid arthritis are generally effective in treating osteoarthritis.
items.explanation: Trials of biologic and disease-modifying agents used for rheumatoid arthritis have mostly been negative for OA.

items.subtopic: RISK FACTORS IN THE JOINT ENVIRONMENT
items.assertion: Weakness in the quadriceps muscles increases the risk of developing painful osteoarthritis in the knee.
items.reason: This is especially true for women, where muscle weakness contributes to joint instability and increased stress on the knee.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Osteoarthritis · RISK FACTORS IN THE JOINT ENVIRONMENT

items.assertion: Comprehensive therapy for osteoarthritis involves a multimodality approach.
items.reason: This includes physical modalities and pharmacologic elements to address pain, weakness across the joint, and laxity and instability.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Weakness in muscles that bridge osteoarthritic joints increases susceptibility to further joint damage and pain.
items.reason: Muscle strength is critical for protecting the joint, and weakness correlates with increased pain and physical limitation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Tai chi is strongly recommended for patients with hand osteoarthritis.
items.reason: There is strong evidence supporting therapeutic exercise benefits in hand OA.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; there is no strong evidence that tai chi or other exercises benefit hand OA.

items.assertion: Celecoxib at low doses is associated with an elevated cardiovascular risk.
items.reason: Low doses of celecoxib ($ ≤ $200 mg/d) are not associated with an elevation of cardiovascular risk.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason correctly states that low doses of celecoxib do not elevate cardiovascular risk.

items.assertion: Hyaluronic acid injections are highly effective in treating knee and hip OA.
items.reason: Most evidence suggests hyaluronic acid injections have little efficacy versus placebo.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, as the reason correctly indicates that hyaluronic acid injections generally show little efficacy.

items.assertion: Duloxetine is an effective treatment option for knee pain in osteoarthritis.
items.reason: Duloxetine is FDA-approved for OA and may be particularly efficacious when knee pain is part of a syndrome of widespread pain.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why duloxetine is effective.
